## 2023-datasette-ecosystem.md

# Datasette: An ecosystem of tools for exploring data and collaborating on data projects

## Simon Willison, NICAR 2023, 4th March 2023

[bit.ly/datasette-nicar-2023](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1jkNEf_YxX7_lvNAaI9G4pYhxpfd2gJHZYtW0CeT6piA/edit?usp=sharing)

Goals for this workshop:

* Provide a higher level tour of the Datasette ecosystem of tools  
* Set you up to run Datasette and learn how to use it  
* Discuss how Datasette can be useful in a newsroom environment  
* Introduce the new preview of Datasette Cloud

[Add questions here (and throughout the document)](#q&a)

[Some demos](#some-demos)

[NICAR in your calendar](#nicar-in-your-calendar)

[Explore the training data behind Stable Diffusion](#explore-the-training-data-behind-stable-diffusion)

[Scottish railway announcements](#scottish-railway-announcements)

[Options for running Datasette yourself](#options-for-running-datasette-yourself)

[On your own machine](#on-your-own-machine)

[On GitHub Codespaces](#on-github-codespaces)

[On a Mac with Datasette Desktop](#on-a-mac-with-datasette-desktop)

[Learn to use the Datasette interface](#learn-to-use-the-datasette-interface)

[Building a SQLite database](#building-a-sqlite-database)

[Publishing data](#publishing-data)

[Datasette Cloud](#datasette-cloud)

[In your browser with Datasette Lite](#in-your-browser-with-datasette-lite)

[The four legs of Datasette](#the-four-legs-of-datasette)

[What’s next?](#what’s-next?)

[Work with me\!](#work-with-me!)

[Bonus section](#bonus-sections-\(didn’t-cover-these-in-the-session\))

[Django SQL Dashboard](#django-sql-dashboard)

[Observable Notebooks](#observable-notebooks)

# Some demos

More at [https://datasette.io/examples](https://datasette.io/examples) 

## NICAR in your calendar

[**https://nicar-2023.vercel.app--/**](https://nicar-2023.vercel.app/) is a little Datasette instance I set up for this event, mainly so I could export the sessions to my calendar.

Subscribe to [https://nicar-2023.vercel.app/nicar2023/calendar.ics](https://nicar-2023.vercel.app/nicar2023/calendar.ics) to get this in your calendar:

This uses the [https://datasette.io/tools/datasette-ics](https://datasette.io/tools/datasette-ics) plugin.

![][image1]

## Explore the training data behind Stable Diffusion

[**http://laion-aesthetic.datasette.io/laion-aesthetic-6pls/images**](http://laion-aesthetic.datasette.io/laion-aesthetic-6pls/images) is a Datasette instance that lets people explore 12m images that were used to train the Stable Diffusion image generation model \- without the consent of the image owners. I built this as part of a collaboration with Andy Baio: [https://waxy.org/2022/08/exploring-12-million-of-the-images-used-to-train-stable-diffusions-image-generator/](https://waxy.org/2022/08/exploring-12-million-of-the-images-used-to-train-stable-diffusions-image-generator/)

![][image2]

This provides a neat demonstration of search and then facet: [http://laion-aesthetic.datasette.io/laion-aesthetic-6pls/images?\_search=obama&\_sort=rowid&\_facet=domain\_id](http://laion-aesthetic.datasette.io/laion-aesthetic-6pls/images?_search=obama&_sort=rowid&_facet=domain_id) 

## Scottish railway announcements

[**https://scotrail.datasette.io/**](https://scotrail.datasette.io/) is an interface for exploring 2,440 automated platform announcements released by ScotRail, the Scottish railway company. It can use a custom SQL query to generate a random apology\!

![][image3]

# Options for running Datasette yourself

There are many options for running Datasette. Let’s do a quick tour.

## On your own machine

Full instructions: [https://docs.datasette.io/en/stable/installation.html](https://docs.datasette.io/en/stable/installation.html) 

If you’re a Python person already, you will know what “pip install datasette” (or “pipx install datasette”) means.

If you have Homebrew, “brew install datasette” works too.

I recommend also installing [sqlite-utils](https://sqlite-utils.datasette.io/en/stable/installation.html) \- a companion tool to Datasette that makes it easy to create and manipulate the SQLite database files that Datasette uses.

Try running this now:

(First, if you are on the lab workshop machines:)

**cd Desktop/hands\_on\_classes/20230304-saturday-datasette**

Now install Datasette:

pipx install datasette

Check that you have Datasette installed:

datasette \--version

And start it running like this:

datasette legislators.db

(If you don’t have that file yet, download it from [https://congress-legislators.datasettes.com/legislators.db](https://congress-legislators.datasettes.com/legislators.db))

Now open [http://127.0.0.1:8001/](http://127.0.0.1:8001/) in your browser to access Datasette.

## On GitHub Codespaces

Installing Python on a personal computer is not as easy as I want it to be\!

A great alternative today is to use [GitHub Codespaces](https://github.com/features/codespaces) instead, which provides a full Linux and Python development environment entirely through your browser. And it has a generous free usage tier (60 hours free each month).

![][image4]

Best thing about Codespaces: if you break it, you can throw it away and start up a new one.

Start here: [https://github.com/simonw/codespaces-datasette](https://github.com/simonw/codespaces-datasette) 

wget [https://congress-legislators.datasettes.com/legislators.db](https://congress-legislators.datasettes.com/legislators.db)  
pipx install datasette  
datasette install datasette-codespaces  
datasette legislators.db

Here’s a full tutorial on using it with Datasette: [**https://datasette.io/tutorials/codespaces**](https://datasette.io/tutorials/codespaces)

## On a Mac with Datasette Desktop

If you’re on a Mac, you can download the Datasette Desktop application. This runs Datasette in a full GUI application, using Electron:

[https://datasette.io/desktop](https://datasette.io/desktop)  
​​  
![][image5]

# Learn to use the Datasette interface

These two tutorials are a great way to get started learning how to use the Datasette interface:

* [**Exploring a database with Datasette**](https://datasette.io/tutorials/explore) shows how to use the Datasette web interface to explore a new database.  
* [**Learn SQL with Datasette**](https://datasette.io/tutorials/learn-sql) introduces SQL, and shows how to use that query language to ask questions of your data.

Both of these tutorials use [https://congress-legislators.datasettes.com/legislators](https://congress-legislators.datasettes.com/legislators) \- a Datasette instance showing members of congress going back to 1789, created using data from the excellent [https://github.com/unitedstates/congress-legislators](https://github.com/unitedstates/congress-legislators) project.

# Building a SQLite database

[https://datasette.io/tutorials/clean-data](https://datasette.io/tutorials/clean-data) \- let’s work through the first few steps:

Grab this: [https://static.simonwillison.net/static/2022/Manatee\_Carcass\_Recovery\_Locations\_in\_Florida.csv](https://static.simonwillison.net/static/2022/Manatee_Carcass_Recovery_Locations_in_Florida.csv)  

pip install sqlite-utils

wget https://static.simonwillison.net/static/2022/Manatee\_Carcass\_Recovery\_Locations\_in\_Florida.csv

sqlite-utils insert \\  
  manatees.db locations \\  
  Manatee\_Carcass\_Recovery\_Locations\_in\_Florida.csv \\  
  \--csv \-d

sqlite-utils schema manatees.db

datasette manatees.db

Hit Ctrl+C to stop the Datasette server, then run:

sqlite-utils transform manatees.db locations \\  
  \--rename LAT latitude \\  
  \--rename LONG\_ longitude \\  
  \--drop created\_user \\  
  \--drop last\_edited\_user \\  
  \--drop X \\  
  \--drop Y \\  
  \--drop STATE \\  
  \--drop OBJECTID \\  
  \--pk FIELDID

sqlite-utils schema manatees.db

datasette manatees.db

Finally we will add a map: hit Ctrl+C and run this:

datasette install datasette-cluster-map

This installs the [datasette-cluster-map](https://datasette.io/plugins/datasette-cluster-map) Datasette plugin.

Start the server running again:

datasette manatees.db

Then visit [http://127.0.0.1:8001/manatees/locations](http://127.0.0.1:8001/manatees/locations) to see the map. Click the blue “load all” button to load all 13,000+ items.

# Publishing data

Datasette was originally designed as a tool for publishing data. The “datasette publish” command can be used to deploy a full copy of the Datasette application along with bundled data (the [Baked Data architectural pattern](https://simonwillison.net/2021/Jul/28/baked-data/)) to a number of different hosting providers:

- Vercel \- good for data less than 50MB  
- Google Cloud Run \- good for larger datasets, up to about 2GB  
- Fly.io \- good for predictable pricing and read-write volumes  
- Heroku

You can also deploy Datasette on a regular Linux server or VPS, or use Docker or Kubernetes \- see [https://docs.datasette.io/en/stable/deploying.html](https://docs.datasette.io/en/stable/deploying.html)

Coming soon: a “how to deploy using Vercel” tutorial. Keep an eye on [https://datasette.io/tutorials](https://datasette.io/tutorials) for that.

# Datasette Cloud

As you can see, I’ve tried a lot of different ways of packaging Datasette so that people can use it\! Datasette Cloud is the latest, and also represents a way for the project to become financially sustainable.

[https://www.datasette.cloud/login/](https://www.datasette.cloud/login/) 

Once you have created an account (I recommend signing in with Google, but you can create a username and password if you don’t want to do that) you’ll need an invite code to create a “space” within Datasette Cloud.

For this event, the invite code is **nicar-2023**

A **space** is a private instance of Datasette. You can upload data to it, and you can also invite other team members to collaborate with you within that space.

You can upload CSV data to Datasette Cloud, but a more exciting way to import data is to pull it directly from open government data portals that run using Socrata (a common platform for those portals).

![][image6]

Try using “Load data from an open data portal” and paste in this URL, to load facilities owned by the city of San Francisco:

[https://data.sfgov.org/City-Infrastructure/City-Facilities/nc68-ngbr](https://data.sfgov.org/City-Infrastructure/City-Facilities/nc68-ngbr) 

Also try this one, which loads supplier contracts for the city:

[https://data.sfgov.org/City-Management-and-Ethics/Supplier-Contracts/cqi5-hm2d](https://data.sfgov.org/City-Management-and-Ethics/Supplier-Contracts/cqi5-hm2d)

Try out the cog menu on the table page:

![][image7]

[https://simonwillison.net/2021/Jul/28/baked-data/](https://simonwillison.net/2021/Jul/28/baked-data/)

# In your browser with Datasette Lite

[https://lite.datasette.io/](https://lite.datasette.io/) is a version of Datasette that runs entirely in your browser, using Python compiled to WebAssembly.

You can use it to open CSV files from the web, provided they have open CORS headers. Everything on GitHub has CORS headers\!

Try clicking the “Load CSV” button and pasting in the URL to a CSV file you find on GitHub, e.g. this one:

[https://github.com/unitedstates/congress-legislators/blob/main/scripts/archive/election\_results\_2022.csv](https://github.com/unitedstates/congress-legislators/blob/main/scripts/archive/election_results_2022.csv) 

In Datasette Lite that becomes:

[https://lite.datasette.io/?csv=https://github.com/unitedstates/congress-legislators/blob/main/scripts/archive/election\_results\_2022.csv](https://lite.datasette.io/?csv=https://github.com/unitedstates/congress-legislators/blob/main/scripts/archive/election_results_2022.csv) 

![][image8]

Since there’s no server-side component to this at all (it’s just static HTML and JavaScript served by GitHub) there’s very little that can go wrong. This is a very robust free way to share a link to a Datasette interface on top of some data.

More on Datasette Lite: [https://simonwillison.net/tags/datasettelite/](https://simonwillison.net/tags/datasettelite/) 

# The four legs of Datasette

- Datasette \- the core application, launched with a CLI or hosted on a server  
- Datasette Desktop \- an installable desktop (Electron) application  
- Datasette Lite \- Datasette entirely in your browser via WebAssembly  
- Datasette Cloud \- the SaaS hosted version

# What’s next?

In terms of features:

* **Datasette 1.0**: stable, documented JSON API and template context  
* **Write API**: Automatically populate your Datasette instance via a secure API \- ideal for running in GitHub Actions and combining with tricks such as [git scraping](https://simonwillison.net/2020/Oct/9/git-scraping/).  
* **Enrichments**: a plugin-based mechanism for “enriching” data \- select a column of addresses and hit “geocode”, select a column of URLs to PDF files and hit “run OCR”, select a column of URLs to YouTube videos and hit “extract transcripts”.  
* **Your feature here**. I want this set of tools to be as useful as possible for investigative reporting. If there’s a feature you need, I really want to hear about it.

## Work with me\!

Most importantly: I came to NICAR with the goal of setting up **direct collaborations with newsrooms**. Datasette is ready. I want to work directly with your newsroom to help you make the most out of the software, and to help me figure out what needs improving and what I should build next.

# Q\&A

Could you explain about 8001 in Code Space?

- When you run a web server it runs on a “port” \- mainly so that you can run more than one server on a single computer. Datasette uses port 8001 by default. Codespaces has a mechanism that spots when a server starts listening on a port and generates a URL which routes traffic to that port.

Does it work every time a server wouldn’t open?

- You can only run one server on each port \- you can use “datasette my.db \-p 8002” to start a server running on port 8002 if port 8001 is already taken.

Are plugins/configuration always global or can I have local configuration for a project?

- Plugins are global \- if you “datasette install datasette-cluster-map” it will be installed for anything else using that Datasette installation. BUT… if you know how to use Python [virtual environments](https://realpython.com/python-virtual-environments-a-primer/) you can run a separate environment for each of your Datasette projects and have different plugins installed in each one.

Can Datasette search multiple tables?

- There are plugins for that\! [https://datasette.io/plugins/datasette-search-all](https://datasette.io/plugins/datasette-search-all) adds an interface for searching across every table that has search configured for it. You can see a demo of that here: [https://fara.datasettes.com/-/search?q=manafort](https://fara.datasettes.com/-/search?q=manafort)  
- Another option is [https://datasette.io/plugins/dogsheep-beta](https://datasette.io/plugins/dogsheep-beta) \- this is a search engine for Datasette that takes some additional configuration but can be used to provide a single search interface across multiple tables, as demonstrated here: [https://datasette.io/-/beta?q=plugins](https://datasette.io/-/beta?q=plugins) 

# Bonus sections (didn’t cover these in the session)

## Django SQL Dashboard

[Django SQL Dashboard](https://django-sql-dashboard.datasette.io/) is a subset of Datasette built for Django projects that use PostgreSQL. If you have an existing PostgreSQL database and a Django application this will give you some of the useful features of Datasette against your existing database.

![][image9]

## Observable Notebooks

For advanced visualizations I like to use [Observable Notebooks](https://observablehq.com/).

Since it’s easy to get JSON or CSV data out of Datasette by URL you can build notebooks that use data from it.

[https://observablehq.com/@simonw/datasette-downloads-per-day-with-observable-plot](https://observablehq.com/@simonw/datasette-downloads-per-day-with-observable-plot) is an example of a notebook I built like this.

[https://observablehq.com/@ambassadors/datasette-client](https://observablehq.com/@ambassadors/datasette-client) provides a deeper integration between Datasette and Observable built by Alex Garcia.  


[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image2]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image3]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image4]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image5]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image6]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image7]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image8]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image9]: <data:image/png;base64,>

## 2023-journalism-workflow-hacks.md

Workflow Hacks & Tips
NICAR23

Who’s who?
Mike Stucka
(formerly of Gannett)

Amir Khafagy
Documented NY
Natalie Lung
Bloomberg News
Geoff Hing
The Marshall Project
Cecilia Reyes
Insider

(Stucka) Planning to plan
Everything gets backed up regularly
Outline what you need to do
Outline your code
Get physical -- physical distance, physical removal from computers -- to think better
Track so you know what decisions, records and progress are missing

Regular backups
Your original data must be sacrosanct. Make a copy out of email. Then make a copy of that.
If you’re putting more than 20 minutes into something, you should put it on Github.
Make incremental backups of projects, with timestamps in the filename.


Outline your code (and your data model)
Spend more time than you want to thinking about what you’re going to do, and you won’t regret it.
Sketch out each section of your code
Think about your data models.
Think about your data models more.
Determine how things relate.
Review what bridges you need to make things work.
Think what you can recycle into this project
Think what this project can get you for future ones

Remove yourself
Change of scenery
Remove distractions
Build a zone
Paper notes

Track everything for accountability
What you need to complete projects
Data peculiarities and definitions
Decision points
Missing records
Missing decisions
Phone calls
Dodge accountability
Tell your editor what you’re working on, but not everything. 

	
Building a pipeline and UI to publish election race call headlines

Real-time results dashboard in a local spreadsheet!
Excel “magic”:
-Macros 
(for auto refresh, sound alerts)
-Conditional highlights
-Formulas

Working with APIs
-Understand what data it spits out
-Read through the API documentation to understand field definitions and possible scenarios, edge cases (so you can anticipate all sorts of possible values that the API spits out)
-Free tools available to help you understand data structure, e.g. https://jsonformatter.org/json-pretty-print

2. Examine real-time updated API/csv data in Excel
-You can input URL of source data, or link a local file and have Excel load it in tabular format
-Get most up-to-date data using Refresh

3. To manage tools development, I found Trello most useful
TRACK AND PRIORITIZE:
-My TODOs (must-have features identified for the pipeline and Excel UI)
-My questions for users / clarifications
-Bugs during testing
-Add-on requests from users (nice-to-haves)

For the end users:
-Make checklists and guidelines for using the tool to reduce friction + increase adoption


TESTING FOR SUCCESS:
-Always ask: “What if…?” “What can possibly break this?”
-Always think of edge cases (numbers: lower bound, upper bound, zeros; elections: wins/losses/tie/retraction) and where systems might fail
-Document for posterity
https://trello.com/b/gTcGPrpv/2022-primaries-midterms

The way things go

Goals for my workflow
Reproducibility
Shared practice
Avoid rabbit holes
Reps on writing clearly about data

When I sit down at my keyboard
Define a list of tasks across projects in a daily note
Further define a task within a project in a data diary entry
Do the task
Quickly inspect new data with VisiData
Write a script/Makefile rule to process data
Analyze data in an executable notebook
File a records request
Follow up with an agency about a data finding
Document what I learned from the task
Note the next step

Daily log
Helps me plan my day
Reminds me what I did last week/last month/3 months ago
Can reflect multiple projects and things that aren't reporting

Home base for project work is a really long Markdown file
Lives in a project folder with analysis scripts
Editor features help make it navigable despite length

Data diary entry
Defines more precisely what I'm going to do
Reminds me what I did in the past in greater detail
Helps others reproduce or validate my work
Will reference or link to scripts, Makefile targets, analysis notebooks, records request trackers, clips or 

Data source documentation
Tells me where to find source data
Reminds me of things that stood out when I first inspected data
Warns me of ambiguities I need to resolve …
… or traps to avoid
Gets copied into analysis notebooks

Zotero notes
Ties source document and takeaway
Linked from data diary, analysis notebook and, eventually draft

Records request and communications tracker

Makefile target
Orchestrates scripts to process data
Provides data that is smaller to analysis notebooks
Simplifies analysis notebooks

Analysis notebook
Starts as an outline of data questions
Evolves into a list of data findings with proof
Draft footnotes link here for fact-checking

*Do* repeat yourself
Use the tool for the job
Link between things when possible
Reference items that can't be linked with easily searchable identifiers such as citations
Copy and paste if there's not an easier alternative
It's ok for some information to become outdated, but pay attention to which places need to be high fidelity (e.g. analysis notebooks)
Having to re-write the same thing helps hone the language about data process and findings

Documenting my process parallels writing about data for an audience
How did I get get here?
Why does it matter?
What do I know?
What do I want to know?
What is the first, next step?
I find that when I describe my data reporting process for co-reporters and editors, and for my future self, some of the things I want to convey to them, are the same things I want to convey to readers when I write about findings and methodology. Ultimately, it's all about trust in the rigor of reporting.

	
When I start work, I…
Update a “log” and generate TODOs as needed
If existing project, link up research, information received

Versions of what I need (not tool specific)
Reminder function
Toggles / some way to hide/show info
Separate FOIA tracking


When I start a project
Running questions
One page - ReadMe, roadmap 
Should answer my high-level questions about the topic
Links, links everywhere
Clips
Timeline (if helpful)
Reminders / memos
Actionable items


DEMO time!

Questions?
Natalie Lung
Email: natlungfy@gmail.com
Twitter: @natlungfy

Cecilia Reyes
reporter.creyes@gmail.com
Twitter: @kcecireyes

Mike Stucka
Email: stucka@gmail.com
Twitter: @MikeStucka
Amir Khafagy
Email: 
Twitter: 

Geoff Hing
Email: geoffhing@gmail..com
Mastodon: @geoffhing@mastodon.social
Twitter: @geoffhing

## 2023-openrefine-powerwash/README.md

# Learning to love OpenRefine

![OpenRefine is shiny and brilliant like a lil blue diamond](https://images.g2crowd.com/uploads/product/image/social_landscape/social_landscape_7bbddfe86a11f15ceb710ca21da17b97/openrefine.jpg)

<h2> OpenRefine is shiny and brilliant, just like its logo. </h2> 

And it can help you clean big datasets even if you don't know how to code!

Objectives of the class: 
- Learn how to open a CSV file in OpenRefine 
- Learn how to use OpenRefine to pivot data and make it easier to work with
- Learn faceting, clustering
- Export cleaned data
- Fall in love <3 

First, let's navigate to OpenRefine's main page. 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1mAeF79tvCyM7j2bb_re3PAFrlZKherP4"></a>

You should have the data stored to your computers, so click the `Choose Files` button.

The .CSVs we will be using are in the `data` folder of this repository. Let's start with the first, `drugsbyhood_spread.csv`, a spreadsheet that shows the number of drug-related incidents reported to San Francisco police by year and police district. 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1MgEIvYPem_r5IYXfGRItI1eBF4Lm5TH7"></a>

Go ahead and click `Next.`

You will hopefully be taken to a window that looks a lil something like this:

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1Vld9F26ouEIuHBtO17G46G7O67Lw6CqU"></a>

Hmmm, that data looks nice and neat. Go ahead and select the `Attempt to parse cell text into numbers` box and then click the <b>`Create Project`</b> button in the top right corner. 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1-svLXKxNKKAPOkokkKvUrUVBlfhzamE5"></a>

<h2> Voila. </h2> 

We are now officially in data-editing land. Your numbers should appear green, but otherwise the table should look similar to how it looked before.

<h1> Part 1: Changing data from wide to long. </h1>

This data is in a table, which is fine, if you like tables. But what if you want the data lined up so that the years are in their own columns? Perhaps you are a data reporter needing to make a specific kind of chart in DataWrapper that requires the years be in columns, for instance.

This task would be kind of a headache if you did it by hand (and what if we had data on 100 years?!) So let's tell OpenRefine to do it instead, in a few easy steps. 

First, click on the triangle next to the `2003` column name. Then select `Transpose` and then `Transpose Cells Across Columns into Rows.`

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1UFh3Oursm6IdfbRLYLmBKW2Eajdewc7n"></a>

This will "pivot" the data from individual columns for each year to putting all of the years into one column and creating a bunch more rows, essentially making it longer and thinner.  If this wording doesn't feel super intuitive yet, don't worry. It's quite common to feel that way.

But before we pivot, let's make sure the data comes out looking the way we want it to. Make sure the box marked `Fill down in other columns` is checked, and select `2003` to `(last column)` to make sure you're transposing all of the year columns into a single column. 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1yPU5nEucXv4bel7VHKyrYcD9iD9I4nND"></a>

Give your `Key Column` the name `year`, and your `Value Column` the name `drug_incidents` or `incidents` or whatever you'd like. Then click `Transpose.`

Your data should now look like this:

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1p6dh-DkXHOQ7OyNA9IPSOw39uH0MmakP"></a>

Now let's switch it back, because why not?! Click the triangle next to the `year` column and select `Columnize by Key/Value Columns.` The following window will pop up.

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1cbbL3R33q_ZD85fHskbgCbdONd3ParXy"></a>

We want to "columnize" the `year` column, and make sure the numbers in the `drug_incidents` column fill in the values for each year, so this looks good! Go ahead and click OK.

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=10VpcUYkfoxVgQ4zaAftKRyE8upeSFf39"></a>

There we go, back to wide format. For fun, try `sort`ing the neighborhoods by order of most reported drug incidents in 2018. Click the drop-down menu next to the `2018` column, then `Sort` and then `Sort...` 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1z-SBKw2jeelauD-b4kQqiODE9k1f1LXZ"></a>

You can now sort the data as numbers, with the largest number appearing first.

Cool! That is the end of part I.

<h1> Part 2: Cleaning dirty data </h1>

A lot of the stuff we just did can be done in Google Spreadsheets pretty easily. But this next part, which will involve cleaning dirty data, is OpenRefine's superpower. While I generally use simple R commands to pivot my data, I still will often run data through OpenRefine before I open it in RStudio because it is very smart at recognizing patterns in cells that might take me longer to address in an R script. 

We are going to practice these skills with a spreadsheet called `ucb_stanford_2014.csv`. This data is an ideal level of messy for this class, so props to Peter Aldhous, my former professor, for digging it up (and for teaching me much of the steps we will take with the data). This data includes federal government grants to UC Berkeley and Stanford University in 2014, downloaded from <a href="https://www.usaspending.gov/">USASpending.gov.</a> 

Go ahead and click the `Open...` button in the top right corner of your OpenRefine window. That should take you back to the OpenRefine landing page. Then click `Choose Files` again, and this time, select `ucb_stanford_2014.csv`. Go ahead and hit `next`. Make sure to click `Attempt to parse cell text into numbers` so that columns with numeric values get recognized as such. 

After configuring that setting, click `Create Project`. Your window should look like this:

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1SMdDg2lkvOkNKx-HrUoxZzgwHHME55I2"></a>

Now the real fun begins. 

<h2> Dealing with additional characters </h2>

Right off the bat, we can see the money column is a bit messy. Some of the values have parentheses to indicate they are negative amounts, and some have commas while others do not. These characters and the $ symbols are making the column get recognized as a text column rather than as numeric, which we'll need if we want to calculate donation totals. 

There's a fancier way to do this, but for now, you can just click on the drop-down arrow next to `Award Amount,` select `Edit Cells` and then click `Replace.` Type in an opening parenthesis `(` into the `Find` box and then in the `Replace` box, type in a `-` dash. Then hit ok. 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=19HINJxs4l076WOxxStzSIgDfmzXXYt9Q"></a>

Then get rid of the other three annoying characters, `)` `,` and `$`, by typing them into the `Find` box and then leaving the `Replace` box blank. Once you do this, you can once again select `Edit Cells,` but this time select `Common Transforms` and then click `To Number.`  Your final column should look like this:

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1eCnUIysQPaVAd9heCYAZNKVfjwruGYJg"></a>

<h2> Clustering </h2>

Let's see if we can group by recipient. Select the drop-down menu in the `Recipient` column and then click `Facet` -> `Text Facet.` Your page should now have a bar on the left that looks like this:

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1rPkXpT83xxl4ylHWbuFUcVuH-yYEcWdC"></a>

Weird. Why are there so many different possible values for `recipient`? There are 28, to be precise, yet we know that the data should only include contributions made to two distinct institutions: Stanford and Berkeley. So let's clean this column up by grouping together values we know to be similar, through a method known as clustering. 

Clustering is my favorite function within OpenRefine, because it can allow you to quickly clean up a big dataset by identifying similar text values without a bunch of fancy fuzzy matching or other complex processes. To me, it is what distinguishes OpenRefine from another low-coding spreadsheet program like Excel. Let's explore the different types of clustering we can do here.

OpenRefine uses two overarching clustering categories. The first, `key collision,` creates alternate versions of strings that contain only the most meaningful parts (the key part), then buckets the strings together based on whether those keys match (the collision part). The second, `nearest neighbor,` compares all the different strings to each other and bins them when they are similar enough. But nearest neighbor methods take forever for big datasets because each pair needs to be compared to every other pair.

Thus, we are going to focus on `key collision` methods because they tend to be more efficient. The first, `fingerprint,` is the default key collision method, because it tends to make the fewest mistakes (`fingerprint` groups different strings together by removing whitespace, changing all characters to lowercase, removing punctuation and several other intuitive steps.) Let's apply the `fingerprint` method to our dataset.

There's also `metaphone3`, a method that transforms strings into how they are typically pronounced. This is useful for errors that involve misspellings. For a more detailed explanation on clustering methods, including a detailed breakdown of `nearest neighbor` methods, see <a href="https://github.com/tfmorris/OpenRefine/wiki/Clustering-In-Depth"> this link. </a>

Let's start with the `fingerprint` method. What does it show? 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1oTpCeGEb1NFcIdN4Tybco28ZcUVBzHh3"></a>

Nice, look like it's accurately, effectively binned some similar values. Go ahead and rename these so they will be grouped together; the `REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA` cluster and the 'University of California, Berkeley' cluster can both become `University of California, Berkeley,` while the `LELAND STANFORD JUNIOR UNIVERSITY` cluster can just become `Stanford University.` Make those changes by entering the text in the corresponding boxes, checking the <b>`Merge?`</b> box and hitting <b>`Merge selected & Re-Cluster.`</b>

That should get rid of about nine of the values. Then you'll have the option to re-cluster again; go ahead and do that. Eventually you'll get to a point where you can't cluster with the `fingerprint` method anymore.

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1NBDpEggoom7rRjOq3FDQx1VMTI37eQsS"></a>

Time to select the `metaphone` method! Ooh, nice, look at all of these choices. Go ahead and `cluster` these ones using the same names you selected earlier: `University of California, Berkeley` and `Stanford University`. 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1LwC4jHNRB2EuVEFtEdkU0svcipFPtt1c"></a>

Woohoo, you should be down to six different types now! From here, close your cluster window and go back to the text facet pane. It should be easy now to manually edit the remaining entries. By clicking on the `Interuniveristy Center for Japanese Language` row, you can see it's located at Stanford, so you can edit that one to be `Stanford University` as well. Go ahead and click the small `edit` button next to the name in the `Receipient` facet box, and do the same for the remaining values so they all fall under either `University of California, Berkeley` or `Stanford University`.

Voila:

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1LNi6zPTI7rOKSc_W5lk4BYN2KZmvQlXP"></a>

You can go ahead and click `x` on the Recipient facet box. 

<h2> Trimming whitespace and changing cases </h2>

So now our `Recipient` column has been cleaned up, but it looks like some of the other columns need cleaning. Go ahead and create a `Text facet` for `Recipient city.` Oof, that's a lot of possible cities for just two universities!

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=13Lh8yOBcXWtKJgjaon7bJISZiA2xgzfg"></a>

This time, before we cluster, let's do something simpler because the city field is so short. Select the arrow to drop-down the transformation menu, click `Edit cells` -> `Common transforms` -> `Trim leading and trailing whitespace.` This removes any spaces that are in the cells either before or after the city name. 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=12zaE7FWJ8XRpS2NeF9JlXLCH9jchxgvh"></a>

Nice, we've matched a bunch of additional values just by taking some spaces out. Now let's do one other easy transform, `Edit cells` -> `Common transforms` -> `To titlecase.` That matches a bunch of other values. We are left only with truly different cities. We couuld take the next step and try to see whether some of these cities are wrongfully entered — after all, again, two universities should only have two cities — but perhaps we can make that call later because questions remain (for instance, should we refer to Stanford by the city that surrounds it, Palo Alto, or the census-designated place on which the university technically sits, Stanford? For now, just manually edit the `Veterans Bureau Hospi` row, located at Stanford, to be in `Palo Alto` (or `Stanford` if you prefer) and move on. 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1ORowUnO328VlimrEbTNkTgZbenytrN9V"></a>

<h2> Convert fields to dates </h2> 

Looks like we have a column in the data, `Award date`, that should be recognized as a date field — only it's not getting recognized as such! If it were, it would be showing up in green like the other non-text columns in our data. So let's change that. Click on the upside-down triangle next to `Award date`, then click `Edit column` -> `Add column based on this column.` 

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1qvlYfh64tL5yQzBx2Q41kXaOPej8OXi5"></a>

There are some fancy coding things you can do in this box, but for now, let's keep it as is. We just want to keep the date column as text to make sure the column converts accurately. Give your column a name, maybe something like `Award Date 2` or `Award Date converted`, and click `OK`.

Then open the menu next to your duplicated award date column and select `Edit cells` -> `Common transforms` -> `To date`. Now your column is in standard date format! And you can make sure it converted correctly by comparing it to the column just to its left. Looks good to me.

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=19Q2K-8uUN5-w5T-butEygdWGJNq-8dUl"></a>

<h2> Made a mistake? no worries, just undo! </h2>

Let's say I actually don't like the way the date column converted and want to go back to a simpler time, a time where I had just one date column. Or perhaps I want to remember all the things I did to the data step by step from the very beginning. Well, OpenRefine allows you to do that in a controlled and discerning manner by showing all of the steps in the `Undo/Redo` bar. From there, you can go back and undo as many steps as you'd like. Click some of the earlier steps and watch your data get de-cleaned.

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1RZuhicKd2t-EDUVJMZQfgVcy6zWsJ-1A"></a>

<h2> Finally, make your cleaning process repeatable </h2>

This Undo/Redo pane, with all of our steps listed in a linear fashion, is also helpful to use in the future, should we encounter similar data and want to clean it quickly. Click the `Extract...` button and the window `Extract Operation History` should pop up. Check the tasks that you'd want to repeat on similar data that aren't super specific to this dataset. For instance, we'd probably want to keep the transformations we made to the financial award column, the whitespace trimming and the case transformations. 

Open a TextEdit window, or if you have Sublime text or a different text editor you prefer, open that. Then paste the code you generated into a new window and save it with a `.json` extension in an easy-to-remember folder. I saved mine in this GitHub repo!

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1vTMLdmIj-uuQP_OpN2wKWtZz-fNZR6Nc"></a>

Now if you want to apply the same commands to a similar data file, you can! Just hit `Apply`, paste the `.json` script you saved into the window, and watch the replicable magic happen.

<a href="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=view&id=1OZFMQsilGH6G20A8x3sqCWi7Fu1VpFAd"></a>

That's all from me :) Hope you enjoyed the class, and please let me know if you have additional questions about OpenRefine or other stuff that I know anything about. 

<h2>More Resources</h2>

OpenRefine Documentation:
https://docs.openrefine.org/

Book: Using OpenRefine
https://www.packtpub.com/product/using-openrefine/9781783289080

Video Tutorial:
https://www.youtube.com/c/Refinepro/featured

## 2023-regex-for-rest-of-us.md

# Regular expressions for the rest of us

## *Justin Myers (@myersjustinc),* *data journalist, The Associated Press*

# **What is a regular expression?**

A regular expression is a common, compact way to represent some sort of pattern in text.

You can use one to:

* Help you search within a document  
* Reformat or otherwise clean up an inconsistent or “dirty” data set  
* Extract structured data from plain text

Many common programming languages, text editors and search tools support regular expressions, so you can use them in whatever environment you find most comfortable.

# **The building blocks**

As complicated as regular expressions can look, they’re all ultimately made up of smaller parts:

* Literals  
  * Letters and numbers refer to themselves. **`a`** refers to the lowercase letter “a”, **`3`** refers to the numeral “3”, etc.  
* Escaping  
  * A ton of characters in regular expressions have special meanings; to override that meaning for a particular character, put a backslash (**`\`**)before it.  
  * For example, a period (**`.`**) matches any character, but an escaped period (**`\.`**) just matches a literal period character.  
  * Often, putting a backslash before a letter does the opposite of this and adds a special meaning where there wasn’t one before; for example, **`d`** matches just that letter, but **`\d`** is shorthand for “any digit”.  
* Character classes  
  * You can specify a class of characters (a list of specific characters to match) by surrounding them in square brackets; for example, **`[aeiou]`** would match any vowel.  
    * These also can contain one or more ranges, where a range is a set of characters in [consecutive ASCII order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASCII#Printable_characters):  
      * **`[0-9]`** would match any digit.  
      * **`[A-Za-z]`** would match any letter.  
      * **`[a-z_]`** would match any lowercase letter or an underscore.  
    * If a caret (**`^`**) is the first character within the brackets, the entire class is negated; for example, **`[^aeiou]`** matches any character *except* those five vowels.  
    * If you want your class to include a literal caret (rather than negate everything else that’s there), you either can escape it (**`\^`**) or put it somewhere other than the beginning; similarly, if you want to match a literal hyphen (rather than define a range), you can escape it or put it at the beginning or the end of the class so it isn’t between two other characters.  
  * On the extreme end of things, a period (**`.`**) matches any character at all (except line breaks, in most cases).  
  * Some common shorthand for frequently used classes (this might differ between programs):  
    * **`\d`** is equivalent to **`[0-9]`** and matches any digit.  
    * **`\s`** matches whitespace characters, like space and tab.  
    * **`\w`** is equivalent to **`[A-Za-z0-9_]`** and matches “word characters”, which means letters, numbers and underscores.  
* Groups  
  * You can surround a part of a regular expression in parentheses—**`(`** and **`)`**—to treat it as a unit.  
  * One common use for this is to provide alternatives, which are sort of like character classes but for more than one letter at a time;   
    for example, **`this (and|or) that`** uses the pipe character (**`|`**) to provide two options for the word between **`this`** and **`that`**. (Without the parentheses, the pipe would have acted on everything before and after it, so it only would have matched the phrase **`this and`** and the phrase **`or that`**.)  
  * Another is to save those groups so you can refer to them later, such as in the replacement step of a find-and-replace operation. These often are referenced by their position; for example, in **`(\d\d\d)-(\d\d\d)-(\d\d\d\d)`**, you might use **`\1`** to refer to the first three digits, **`\2`** to refer to the second three digits and **`\3`** to refer to the last four digits.  
* Quantifiers  
  * Several symbols exist to let you specify how many times in a row to match a given character, class or group.  
  * A question mark (**`?`**) matches zero or one occurrence; for example, **`colou?r`** makes the **`u`** optional, so it would match either the American spelling **`color`** or the Commonwealth spelling **`colour`**.  
  * An asterisk (**`*`**) matches zero or more occurrences; for example, **`snakes*`** matches **`snake`**, **`snakes`** and **`snakesssssssssss`**.  
  * A plus sign (**`+`**) matches one or more occurrences; for example, **`ba(na)+`** matches **`bana`**, **`banana`** and **`banananananana`**—but it does not match **`ba`** by itself.  
  * A number in braces (**`{}`**) matches only that many occurrences; for example, **`vacu{2}m`** only matches **`vacuum`**, because we specified there had to be two **`u`**’s there. This also means you could rewrite the phone number pattern from earlier (in “Groups”) as **`(\d{3})-(\d{3})-(\d{4})`** and mean exactly the same thing.  
    * The braces also can contain a range of numbers; for example, **`\d{7,10}`** will match any number that’s got seven, eight, nine or 10 digits.  
    * Omitting the number before the comma is the same as putting a zero there; for example, **`A-Z`** matches any sequence of *up to three* capital letters, such as **`X`**, **`AA`** and **`BLS`**.  
    * Omitting the number after the comma is like putting an infinity there; for example, **`Go{2,}gle`** would match **`Google`**, **`Gooooooogle`** and any other form of the word (like at the bottom of Google’s search results), as long as it has *at least two* consecutive **`o`**’s.  
* Anchors  
  * A caret (**`^`**) matches the beginning of a line of text, which can be useful if you’re searching through an entire file, like if you wanted to match the first field in each row of a CSV.  
  * A dollar sign (**`$`**) similarly matches the end of a line of text.  
  * **`\A`** matches the beginning of the entire string you’re searching against, regardless of whether there are multiple lines in it or not.  
  * **`\Z`** does the same thing, but for the end of the entire string.

# **Putting it all together**

Once you have some of those building blocks, you might see a regular expression like this:

**`^(IX|IV|V?I{,3})$`**

This regex is searching for any Roman numeral from one through nine, on a line of text all by itself. How does that work?

First, we know each match must be on its own line. The pattern starts with **`^`**, which means whatever comes after it *must* be at the beginning of a line. It also ends with **`$`**, which means whatever comes before it *must* be at the end of a line.

Between the start and end of the line, we have a group with three alternatives in it, separated by pipes:

* The first alternative is **`IX`**, the roman numeral for nine.  
* The second alternative is **`IV`**, for four.  
* The third alternative covers all of the remaining numerals:  
  * It starts with a **`V`** and makes that optional with a question mark.  
  * Whether that **`V`** is present or not, we then may have up to three **`I`**’s. (So without the **`V`**, this alternative covers the numbers one through three; with the **`V`**, we also get five through eight.)

Note this expression also can match a completely empty line. We could be in the third alternative there, which would match a string with zero **`V`**’s and zero **`I`**’s—which would be nothing\!

*(The expression above is adapted from [chapter 5](https://diveinto.org/python3/regular-expressions.html#verbosere) of [Dive Into Python 3](https://diveinto.org/python3/table-of-contents.html) by Mark Pilgrim.)*

# **Common differences**

Different programming languages, text editors and other tools handle regular expressions in their own ways. Some of the things that differ most often are:

## Character classes

The shorthand for various character classes (all digits, all letters, all whitespace, etc.) can vary between environments. Some don’t support any, so you’d need to define them manually, such as with brackets.

Others support more than just the basic classes like **`\d`** mentioned earlier. Look into “[POSIX character classes](https://www.regular-expressions.info/posixbrackets.html)” for some examples; they tend to look like **`[:alpha:]`**, which matches any letter.

## Escaping

Some environments have different rules about when certain characters do and don’t need to be escaped. For example, Vim requires you to use escaped parentheses—**`\(`** and **`\)`**—to define groups; unescaped parentheses are treated instead as literal characters. Also, some languages [such as R](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/strings.html#basic-matches) require backslashes themselves to be escaped, so you might have to type twice as many backslashes as you otherwise would think.

## Group references

You might need to refer to groups by using dollar signs rather than backslashes; for example, the first group might be represented by **`$1`** instead of **`\1`**.

Sometimes there are ways to assign names to your groups, so you don’t have to use position-based numbers. ([Django’s URL definitions](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/4.1/topics/http/urls/#using-regular-expressions) support a version of this.) The syntax is environment-specific, so look it up first.

## Flags

Some environments support the use of “flags”, which modify the rules of the regular expressions you use. Some common ones:

* “Global” means your expression can match more than one occurrence of the same pattern in the same line or document. This often is implied in text editors, but you might need to specify it when programming.  
* “Multiline” expands the meaning of **`.`** to include line breaks, so you can match pieces of text that span multiple lines.  
* “Verbose” ignores the literal meanings of most whitespace characters that are in your expression, so you can break it up and include comments in order to make it easier to read and debug.

How you enable these depends on your environment, so read up on the language or editor you’ll be using.

# **Environment-specific resources**

Here are some places to find details about the regex implementation in your own project.

## Editors

* BBEdit: [https://gist.github.com/ccstone/5385334](https://gist.github.com/ccstone/5385334)  
* Emacs: [https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/RegularExpression](https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/RegularExpression)  
* Notepad++: [https://npp-user-manual.org/docs/searching/\#regular-expressions](https://npp-user-manual.org/docs/searching/#regular-expressions)  
* Sublime Text: [https://sublime-text-unofficial-documentation.readthedocs.io/en/latest/](https://sublime-text-unofficial-documentation.readthedocs.io/en/latest/search_and_replace/search_and_replace_overview.html#regular-expressions)  
  [search\_and\_replace/search\_and\_replace\_overview.html\#regular-expressions](https://sublime-text-unofficial-documentation.readthedocs.io/en/latest/search_and_replace/search_and_replace_overview.html#regular-expressions)  
* Vim: [https://vim.fandom.com/wiki/Search\_patterns](https://vim.fandom.com/wiki/Search_patterns)  
* Visual Studio Code: [https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editor/codebasics\#\_find-and-replace](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/editor/codebasics#_find-and-replace)

## Languages

* Bash: [https://tldp.org/LDP/abs/html/regexp.html](https://tldp.org/LDP/abs/html/regexp.html)  
* JavaScript: [https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Regular_Expressions)  
  [Guide/Regular\_Expressions](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Regular_Expressions)  
* PHP: [https://php.net/manual/en/book.pcre.php](https://php.net/manual/en/book.pcre.php)  
* Python: [https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html](https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html)  
* R: [https://stringr.tidyverse.org/](https://stringr.tidyverse.org/) or [https://rdrr.io/r/base/grep.html](https://rdrr.io/r/base/grep.html)  
* Ruby: [https://ruby-doc.org/3.2.1/Regexp.html](https://ruby-doc.org/3.2.1/Regexp.html)

# **Other resources**

* RegExr: [https://regexr.com/](https://regexr.com/)  
  * Web page that lets you test out different regular expressions  
  * Annotates each part of your expression, and provides helpful info about each match  
* Regex Crossword: [https://regexcrossword.com/](https://regexcrossword.com/)  
  * Game to help you practice interpreting regular expressions  
* Hands-on page: [http://jmye.rs/nicar-regex](http://jmye.rs/nicar-regex)  
  * Web page used in the NICAR session to practice the basics

## 2023-stats-in-excel.md

**NICAR 2023**

**Excel Basics Stats**

**3/2/2023**

**You CAN do stats in Excel.**

**Todd Wallack**  
**WBUR**  
**twallack@bu.edu**  
**617.291.8123**

**Install Analysis ToolPak as “Add-In”**![][image1]

**Where to find ToolPak function**

**Look for the data menu**

**![][image2]**

**Then, look for the icon all the way on the right**

	

**![][image3]**

**Excel Formulas** 

**![][image4]**

**How to generate all those statistics with the ToolPak**

**![][image5]**

**![][image6]**

**![][image7]**

**Measures of “central tendency”** 

Average/Mean – Most common (Total/\# of items)

Median \-  Middle value. Better to show typical value.

Mode: Value that comes up the most. 

**Measures of dispersion:**

Standard Deviation: How far numbers differ from the median. In a normal curve, 68% of values are within 1 standard deviation. 95% are within 2\. And 99.7% are within three. So a difference of 2 standard deviations is usually significant. Outliers are generally beyond three standard deviations. 

Standard Error: How much numbers of a sample are likely to differ from the true value in the overall population. Bigger samples have lower standard errors.

Range:  Difference between the smallest and largest value

**Other measures:**

Skewness:  Measures the shape of your curve. Positive skewness indicates a distribution with an asymmetric tail extending toward more positive values. Negative skewness indicates a distribution with an asymmetric tail extending toward more negative values.

Kurtosis: A measure of how often outliers occur (at one of the tails). Bigger numbers tend to have long thin tails.

**Rates**

**Per capita rates are “per person” or per unit.**

**Number / Population**

**But you can convert to other rates like…..**

**Per 1,000                                  Value / Population \* 1000**

**Per 100,000		           Value / Population  \*  100,000**

**Best to use a rate that allows you to express numbers more clearly.** 

**Like 15 homicides per 100,000 people. Instead of .00015 gun deaths per person or per capita.**

**Experiment with the rate that works best.**

**![][image8]**

**First, Create bin range.**

**![][image9]**

**Then choose Histogram option in the DataPak**

**![][image10]**

**Select both where the data and bins are located**

**![][image11]**

**Then you can click on the chart and change the labels**

**![][image12]**

**Correlations**

**![][image13]**

**![][image14]**

**Example of what it looks like:**

**Correlation is between 0 (weak) and 1 (strong).**

**Negative correlations mean when one value goes up, the other goes down.**

**![][image15]**

**Scatter plot – another way to show correlation**

**![][image16]**

**![][image17]**

**Simple regression (two values)**

**![][image18]**

**Sample regression**

**![][image19]**

**Multiple Regression  (more than 1 factor at once)**

**![][image20]**

**![][image21]**

**![][image22]**

**To compare coefficients to see which one is most important, standardize values.**

**The formula is \=standardize(x,average,stdev)**

**![][image23]**

**Notes about non-numerical columns:**

Regression only works on numerical values, like salaries and height. If you want to convert a categorical variable to numerical, you need to turn it into a numerical value (often called a dummy variable). So for instance, if you have a gender column with categories like male, female and nonbinary,  you might create a new column called “female” that has 1 for female and 0 for not female. Then you can use the new column as a factor in your regression analysis.  Or if you have a column with types of vehicles, you might create a new dummy variable called truck with 1 for trucks and 0 for other kinds of vehicles.

Some of this stuff is complicated, particularly when it comes to regression. Before you publish a story, it often makes sense to contact a statistician or researcher who will look over your analysis and make sure you are not missing anything.
